summary: Paul commands the church to confront open sexual sin, preserve its holiness, and practice accountable fellowship.

outline.title: Confronting Open Sin
outline.summary: Paul commands the church to address blatant immorality for the offender’s ultimate good and the congregation’s holiness.

outline.title: Remove the Old Leaven
outline.summary: Because Christ is the church’s Passover sacrifice, its shared life must be marked by sincerity and truth.

outline.title: Holiness Within the Church
outline.summary: Paul distinguishes ordinary contact with the world from accountable fellowship with someone claiming faith while persisting in serious sin.

verses.bibleText: It is reported commonly that there is fornication among you, and such fornication as is not so much as named among the Gentiles, that one should have his father's wife.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul turns from party spirit to a case of public sexual wrongdoing: a man is living with “his father’s wife,” most naturally his stepmother. The phrasing echoes the biblical prohibition against uncovering a father’s nakedness. Roman law also prohibited such unions, so Paul’s claim that the case exceeded ordinary Gentile tolerance is not mere religious insulation. The relationship was scandalous even by the city’s public standards.

The present-tense expression “that one should have” suggests an ongoing union, not a single past incident. The term translated “fornication” (porneia) is broad sexual-immorality language; its content is defined here by the prohibited family relationship.

It is possible the man’s father was dead, divorced, or still living; the text does not say. Calling the woman “his father’s wife” rather than his mother makes a stepmother likely but does not remove the betrayal of family order.

Paul’s comparison with Gentiles is rhetorical but grounded. Greco-Roman sexual practice could be permissive in several areas, yet incest laws and moralists rejected this relationship. The church’s claim to holiness had been publicly contradicted on terms even neighbors could recognize.

“Reported commonly” indicates that this was no private suspicion. The church was dealing with known, continuing conduct, not an accusation that had yet to be established. Paul names the man but says nothing about disciplining the woman, which may indicate that she was outside the congregation.

The verse should not be generalized into permission to police private lives through rumor. Its setting includes clear covenant standards, an identifiable member, and conduct already public enough to damage both people and the church’s witness.

verses.bibleText: And ye are puffed up, and have not rather mourned, that he that hath done this deed might be taken away from among you.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The congregation’s pride alarms Paul almost as much as the offense. Instead of mourning, they are “puffed up.” The letter does not tell us whether they defended the relationship through a mistaken account of freedom, protected a socially important member, or simply treated tolerance as evidence of sophistication. Any of those motives would fit Corinth; none can be proved.

Mourning is the proper first response because sexual sin wounds persons, families, and the holiness of the community. It excludes the relish with which religious people can expose another’s failure. Discipline that begins in anger or superiority has already missed Paul’s tone.

“Taken away from among you” points toward exclusion, but mourning should accompany every stage. Israel mourned covenant breaches and leaders sometimes grieved when judgment became necessary. Paul expects a corporate sorrow that recognizes the sin as a wound to the body, not only the offender’s private problem.

The church’s pride may also have made the man difficult to confront if he possessed influence. Ancient associations often depended on patrons, and disciplining a benefactor could carry economic cost. That scenario is possible rather than certain, yet Paul’s instruction clearly places holiness above social advantage.

Mourning also guards the innocent. The father and wider household may have suffered betrayal even if they are not mentioned. Ethical reflection should not center the offender so completely that harmed persons disappear.

The required removal is serious, but it is not a declaration that the offender is beyond grace. The church must stop affirming his profession while the conduct continues. That truthful boundary is intended to awaken repentance and protect the congregation from learning that covenant faithfulness is optional.

verses.bibleText: For I verily, as absent in body, but present in spirit, have judged already, as though I were present, concerning him that hath so done this deed,

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Though absent from Corinth, Paul has “judged already” the act under discussion. His decisiveness rests on facts he regards as established and on a moral command that is not ambiguous. He is not choosing between competing personal preferences.

“Present in spirit” expresses real solidarity through the letter and the common life believers share in Christ. It need not imply a mystical journey or disembodied presence. When the congregation hears his words and acts upon them, Paul’s apostolic judgment is effectively among them.

Paul’s phrase “concerning him that hath so done this deed” keeps the verdict attached to an act. He does not pronounce on rumors about character or every part of the person’s history.

His readiness to judge also qualifies the warning in 4:5. Christians may not anticipate Christ’s final disclosure of hidden motives, but they can and must evaluate established conduct under a clear command. Refusing every moral judgment would make protection and restoration impossible.

The standard is translocal. Corinthian enthusiasm does not get to redefine conduct that the apostolic churches receive from Scripture. Yet implementation belongs within a real local assembly, where facts and response can be responsibly known.

The verse does not establish unilateral discipline from a distant leader. Verses 4–5 require the assembled church to participate. Paul supplies apostolic direction; the community must take responsibility for its own integrity.

verses.bibleText: In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, when ye are gathered together, and my spirit, with the power of our Lord Jesus Christ,

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Discipline is to occur “in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ,” when the church is gathered, with Paul’s apostolic witness and Christ’s power. Each phrase limits abuse. The action is not a private vendetta, a secret decision by one influential member, or an attempt to protect institutional reputation.

Christ’s name identifies the authority and character under which the church acts. His power is not coercive force but his effective lordship among a people obeying his word. The congregation must therefore pursue truth, fairness, holiness, and restoration.

The syntax of verses 3–5 is unusually long, and translations differ over where “in the name of our Lord Jesus” attaches. It most naturally characterizes the assembly or the act of delivering. Either way, Jesus’ authority surrounds the whole process.

Paul’s “spirit” present with them can also express the unity of judgment between apostle and church. It should not be used as a model for claims that an absent leader knows hidden local facts supernaturally.

Congregational involvement distributes responsibility but does not turn discipline into a popular vote unconstrained by evidence. The gathered church acts under Christ’s word, with leaders facilitating a process that the body can understand and own.

Public, established sin requires accountable communal action. Even then, confidentiality should be preserved as far as faithfulness allows, and vulnerable people must be protected. Gathering in Christ’s name never excuses humiliation for its own sake.

verses.bibleText: To deliver such an one unto Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord Jesus.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Deliver such an one unto Satan” describes removal from the covenant fellowship into the world where rebellion holds sway. Paul is not authorizing violence, a magical curse, or manipulative deliverance practices. The church acknowledges that a person persisting in this conduct cannot at the same time enjoy the community’s public affirmation as a faithful brother.

Satan is named as the ruler of the rebellious sphere outside Christ’s covenant community, not as a partner whose intentions become benevolent. God may use the painful consequences of exclusion against Satan’s own purposes, just as trials can awaken a person without making the tempter holy.

The day of the Lord supplies the horizon. Discipline is judged by whether it serves a person’s final reconciliation to Christ and the community’s fidelity—not whether it satisfies public anger. If repentance comes, prolonged exclusion can become disobedient by refusing the very end Paul sought.

The difficult phrase “for the destruction of the flesh” may refer to the breaking of sinful desire through the shock of exclusion; some have understood physical affliction. Paul does not explain the mechanism, so the church must not manufacture one. The stated aim is restorative: “that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord Jesus.” He hopes discipline will lead to repentance and final salvation.

Restoration also requires a church ready to receive the repentant. If the community uses exclusion to define itself as pure, the offender may find no path home even after the fleshly pattern is broken. Paul’s later counsel in 2 Corinthians 2—to forgive and comfort a disciplined person so sorrow does not overwhelm him—shows the pastoral rhythm, whether or not it concerns the same case. Boundary and welcome answer different moments in one redemptive purpose.

This wording does not teach that an immortal soul is saved while the body is discarded. Paul’s hope elsewhere is the salvation of the whole person in the resurrection. “Flesh” can name the rebellious orientation that must die, while “spirit” can describe the person turned again toward God. Exclusion is a grave medicinal judgment, never permission to stop seeking the offender’s repentance and eventual restoration.

wordNotes.explanation: Here likely the rebellious orientation and desires that discipline is intended to break. The term does not make the physical body evil or describe an immortal soul escaping a disposable body.

verses.bibleText: Your glorying is not good. Know ye not that a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The church’s “glorying” is dangerous because tolerated evil does not remain neatly contained. A small amount of leavened dough, retained from an earlier batch, could work through a new lump. The metaphor emphasizes quiet, pervasive influence rather than numerical size.

Paul does not say that contact with a sinner mechanically contaminates believers; verses 9–10 reject that idea. His concern is what communal acceptance teaches. If public defiance is treated as compatible with faithful membership, conscience is retrained and others are encouraged to test the same boundary.

Leaven in this setting was not a packet of commercial yeast. A piece of fermented dough from an earlier batch was worked through new dough. Its smallness made its eventual spread more vivid.

The metaphor should not produce suspicion that every struggling member will contaminate the church. Paul addresses prideful tolerance of known, settled conduct. Churches are hospitals for repentant sinners; discipline distinguishes that repentance from defiance.

“Know ye not?” implies they already understand the domestic fact and should recognize its moral analogy. Paul often begins correction by recalling knowledge they possess but have failed to integrate. Information without faithful judgment has become another form of immaturity.

Leaven imagery is powerful but can be abused if a church labels disliked people “contamination.” Paul identifies a public practice and the congregation’s boasting, not ethnicity, disability, poverty, questions, or the presence of repentant strugglers. The church’s danger is moral normalization. Compassionate proximity to weakness is part of its calling; arrogant acceptance of harm is what works through the lump.

The warning applies to more than sexual wrongdoing. Boasting, greed, idolatry, abusive speech, and exploitation appear in the chapter’s later list. A church cannot preserve its health by disciplining conspicuous sins while allowing respectable forms of corruption to ferment unchecked.

verses.bibleText: Purge out therefore the old leaven, that ye may be a new lump, as ye are unleavened. For even Christ our passover is sacrificed for us:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Purge out therefore the old leaven” draws on preparations for Passover, when leaven was removed from Israelite homes. Paul immediately identifies the controlling event: “Christ our passover is sacrificed for us.” Jesus’ death is the decisive deliverance from bondage and judgment to which Israel’s exodus pointed.

The command rests on identity: “that ye may be a new lump, as ye are unleavened.” In Christ they already belong to the delivered people; they must remove practices that contradict that gift. Grace precedes cleansing and makes cleansing necessary.

Passover began with deliverance initiated by God. Israel did not remove leaven in order to persuade God to rescue it; the household obeyed within the rescue God announced. Paul preserves that grace-shaped order: Christ has been sacrificed, therefore remove the old leaven.

Calling the church “unleavened” is an indicative of identity, not a denial that sin remains. They are the new people constituted by Christ’s exodus. The command asks the visible congregation to correspond to that reality.

The metaphor also joins individual and corporate holiness. One person’s conduct matters to the whole lump, while the community bears responsibility to act without pretending it can manufacture purity by scapegoating one offender.

“Christ our Passover” joins substitution and liberation. The sacrificed lamb’s blood marked households spared from judgment, and the exodus freed a people for God. Christ’s death likewise deals with guilt and breaks bondage. A church invoking the sacrifice while protecting oppressive conduct has accepted one half of the image and denied the other: the Passover people must leave the old slavery behind.

The aorist “was sacrificed” anchors identity in a completed historical event. The Corinthians do not reenact the sacrifice by achieving communal purity. They remove leaven because the sacrifice has already reconstituted them. Any discipline that implies Christ’s work was insufficient or that leaders can provide atonement for the church has lost the metaphor’s order.

The metaphor is corporate. Paul is not urging anxious self-purification as though believers could qualify for Christ’s sacrifice. He calls the church to live truthfully within a redemption already accomplished, using discipline as one part of that faithful response.

wordNotes.explanation: The festival and sacrifice remembering Israel’s deliverance from Egypt. Paul identifies Christ as the decisive Passover sacrifice whose completed deliverance calls the church to a cleansed common life.

verses.bibleText: Therefore let us keep the feast, not with old leaven, neither with the leaven of malice and wickedness; but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Let us keep the feast” extends Passover imagery across Christian life. Paul is not imposing the Jewish festival calendar on Gentile believers or forbidding ordinary leavened bread. Because Christ is the Passover sacrifice, the church’s continuing celebration of deliverance must be marked by a life that corresponds to it.

“Malice and wickedness” are the old leaven; “sincerity and truth” are the unleavened bread. The contrast reaches beyond one sexual case. Malice poisons relationships, and falsehood allows a community to rename disobedience as freedom. Sincerity describes unmixed motive; truth requires that confession and conduct agree with God’s reality.

The present subjunctive “let us keep the feast” can suggest continuing celebration. Early Christians lived from a completed Passover sacrifice while awaiting final deliverance. Every gathering and ordinary act could therefore embody the festival’s truth.

Paul’s pair “sincerity and truth” deserves attention. Sincerity without truth can describe wholehearted error; truth without sincerity can become correct words masking corruption. The gospel seeks an integrity in which motive, confession, and conduct agree before God.

Applied to discipline, this means the church cannot defend its reputation with concealed procedures. It must act truthfully, with unmixed concern for holiness, harmed persons, and the offender’s restoration.

The feast language also prevents discipline from becoming the center of church identity. The center is Christ’s completed sacrifice and the joyful life of truth it creates. Removal of leaven serves celebration. Holiness is not a permanently suspicious posture; it is freedom to worship and live without the duplicity that corrupts fellowship.

Sincerity can carry the image of something tested in sunlight, though its exact etymology should not bear interpretive weight. Context gives the sense of unmixed integrity. “Truth” then keeps sincerity accountable to reality. Together they describe a church that neither conceals its life nor invents kinder names for destructive conduct.

The Lord’s Supper may echo in the background, but the feast cannot be confined to a ritual meal. Worship and daily ethics belong together. A church celebrates the crucified Christ honestly when his deliverance changes how its members live with one another.

verses.bibleText: I wrote unto you in an epistle not to company with fornicators:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul refers to an earlier letter in which he had told them not to associate with sexually immoral people. That letter has not survived. Its mention reminds us that 1 Corinthians belongs to a longer pastoral relationship involving visits, reports, and correspondence.

The lost letter cautions against imagining that the canonical correspondence records every contact or gives a complete chronology. Inspiration preserved what God intended for the church without preserving every apostolic document.

Some have proposed that part of 2 Corinthians contains the earlier letter, but the evidence is debated and unnecessary for interpreting this verse. Paul treats the previous instruction as available to the Corinthians and now corrects their reading.

His clarification models patient teaching. A true command can be implemented wrongly when scope and purpose are misunderstood. Repetition with sharper definition is not compromise.

The Corinthians had apparently misunderstood or misapplied the instruction. Paul now clarifies its scope rather than blaming them for failing to infer every qualification.

“Not to company” means more than an accidental encounter. It describes the fellowship by which a church treats someone as a member in good standing. The distinction matters: holiness requires truthful boundaries inside the covenant community, not fear of ordinary contact with people who do not share its confession.

verses.bibleText: Yet not altogether with the fornicators of this world, or with the covetous, or extortioners, or with idolaters; for then must ye needs go out of the world.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul expressly excludes an isolationist reading. If believers avoided every immoral, greedy, exploitative, or idolatrous person in wider society, they would have to “go out of the world.” The church remains present in neighborhoods, trades, households, and public life where it can bear witness.

Ordinary contact is not moral approval. Christians may work with, eat with, and care for people whose conduct they cannot affirm, while refusing participation in wrongdoing. Jesus’ ministry among sinners supplies the pattern: proximity joined to truth and mercy.

The four categories are representative of ordinary pagan urban life: sexual relationships outside biblical bounds, acquisitive desire, commercial exploitation, and worship of other gods. Avoiding everyone implicated in them would end mission and much daily commerce.

“Go out of the world” is an impossible counterfactual, not a call to establish an isolated Christian settlement. Paul expects believers to remain present without sharing in idolatrous worship or wrongdoing.

That presence requires discernment. Friendship and service can be genuine rather than a disguised recruitment technique, while witness remains honest about disagreement. Holiness is shown by faithful conduct amid the city, not by ignorance of its people.

The list also widens moral vision. Greed and extortion stand beside sexual immorality and idolatry. A congregation that condemns one visible vice while accommodating economic exploitation has not understood Paul’s holiness.

verses.bibleText: But now I have written unto you not to keep company, if any man that is called a brother be a fornicator, or covetous, or an idolater, or a railer, or a drunkard, or an extortioner; with such an one no not to eat.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The boundary concerns anyone “called a brother” whose settled public life is characterized by sexual immorality, greed, idolatry, abusive speech, drunkenness, or extortion. Paul is not demanding sinless perfection before fellowship. The issue is persistent conduct joined to a Christian profession and resistant to correction.

“With such an one no not to eat” includes ordinary table fellowship and would naturally bear on the church’s communal meal as well. In that culture, eating together publicly signaled acceptance and solidarity. Temporary exclusion makes the church’s verdict visible.

“Called a brother” places profession and conduct in direct tension. The church disciplines in relation to the confession a person asks it to recognize. It does not claim authority over everyone who once attended a service or bears a cultural Christian label.

The list’s economic terms matter in Corinth. A covetous or extortionate member might use contracts, loans, patronage, or courts while maintaining a respectable sexual reputation. Paul gives the congregation no permission to ignore such harm.

Eating signaled more than caloric intake; table fellowship expressed social belonging. Exclusion therefore carried real loneliness and shame. Its seriousness requires that lesser measures—private appeal, instruction, and opportunities to repent—be pursued where appropriate before the final boundary.

This command must be handled consistently and with due process. Wealth, family influence, and respectable appearance cannot shield the greedy or abusive while only less powerful offenders are disciplined. When repentance occurs, the same gospel that required a boundary requires forgiveness and restoration.

verses.bibleText: For what have I to do to judge them also that are without? do not ye judge them that are within?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul sharply limits the church’s jurisdiction: “what have I to do to judge them also that are without?” The congregation is responsible for those who claim its fellowship, not for acting as a tribunal over everyone in the city.

“Do not ye judge them that are within?” refers to accountable moral discernment, not contemptuous condemnation. A covenant community must be able to say when public conduct contradicts its confession; otherwise membership communicates nothing.

The inside–outside distinction concerns discipline, not compassion or moral truth. God’s commands remain good for all people, and the gospel calls outsiders to repentance. The church simply lacks covenant standing to impose its membership judgments on those who do not belong to it.

Paul’s question also reorders public witness. A community loses credibility when it campaigns against the city while excusing greed and abuse among its own leaders. Transparent internal accountability is itself part of mission.

Judging “within” should include judging systems as well as isolated acts. If congregational habits protect influential offenders or silence the harmed, correcting one individual will not fulfill Paul’s demand.

The distinction corrects two opposite failures. The church must not abandon internal discipline while directing moral outrage at outsiders, and it must not use concern for grace as an excuse to ignore harm within. Its most immediate field of accountability is its own common life.

verses.bibleText: But them that are without God judgeth. Therefore put away from among yourselves that wicked person.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Them that are without God judgeth.” Outsiders are not beyond God’s moral authority, but their final judgment is God’s work. The church witnesses, serves, reasons, and calls people to reconciliation; it does not enforce covenant discipline on those who never claimed the covenant.

The final command in Deuteronomy normally followed a judicial process aimed at removing covenant evil. Paul transfers its covenant logic, not Israel’s civil method, into the church. The congregation’s available action is exclusion from its fellowship.

“God judgeth” can be read as a present principle or a future certainty. Either way, outsiders are not forgotten. Divine judgment relieves the church of coercive ambition while preserving moral seriousness.

The chapter ends without narrating the man’s response. Christian readers must live within that unfinished appeal: take sin seriously, enact discipline justly, seek repentance, and be ready to restore. Any one of those elements isolated from the others distorts the apostolic purpose.

“Put away from among yourselves that wicked person” echoes repeated commands in Deuteronomy to remove evil from Israel. Paul applies covenant language to the Gentile-majority church, identifying it as a holy people under God’s word. The Christian form of removal is exclusion from fellowship, not Israel’s civil penalties and never physical harm.

The command is the sorrowful last step appropriate to established, serious, unrepentant conduct. Its purpose remains the protection of the community and the offender’s awakening. God’s judgment gives discipline its gravity; God’s mercy gives it its hope.
